title: INF10025 Lecture 8 - Part 3
summary: INF10025Data Management and AnalyticsLecture 8 – Part 3 Modelling M:M relationships SQL: Outer Joins, SubQueries, Unions, Constraints SQL: UNION & UNION ALL The UNION operators combine the results of two queries into a single result set. Th
content_html:
INF10025Data Management and AnalyticsLecture 8 – Part 3
Modelling
M:M relationships
SQL: Outer Joins, SubQueries,
Unions, Constraints

SQL: UNION & UNION ALL
The UNION operators combine the results of two queries into a single result set.
The number and data types of the columns selected by each component query must be the same, but the column lengths can be different.
UNION
All rows selected by either query.
UNION ALL
All rows selected by either query, including all duplicates.

SQL: Union
SELECT name FROM STUDENT
UNION
SELECT name FROM TUTOR;
Amanda Allen
Bob Black
Carol Chen
Dave Dole
Ed Ellis
Fred Foley
Names that are in both tables are not duplicated
A Union operator combines results of individual SQL queries to be combined into one single result set
STUDENT
TUTOR
SELECT name FROM STUDENT;Amanda Allen
Bob Black
Carol Chen
Dave Dole
SELECT name FROM TUTOR;Carol Chen
Ed Ellis
Fred Foley
An organisation has details about Students and Tutors
Some people are in both tables

SQL: Union ALL
A Union ALL operator causes ALL rows to be added. Duplicates may occur.
STUDENT
TUTOR
SELECT name FROM STUDENT;Amanda Allen
Bob Black
Carol Chen
Dave Dole
SELECT name FROM TUTOR;Carol Chen
Ed Ellis
Fred Foley
Names that are in both tables are duplicated
SELECT name FROM STUDENT
UNION ALL
SELECT name FROM TUTOR;
Amanda Allen
Bob Black
Carol Chen
Carol Chen
Dave Dole
Ed Ellis
Fred Foley

SQL: Unions: Column types must match
The data types of columns from both select statements       in the Union must match
Length may vary
Precision may vary
Char and Varchar may be mixed
Queries may require use of a conversion function such as     TO_CHAR()     or     CAST() or     CONVERT () – non oracle
TO_NUMBER( string1, [ format_mask ])
TO_NUMBER(amt,'999.99')
TO_CHAR(num-exp)
TO_CHAR(emp_postcode)

SQL: INTERSECT & MINUS
The INTERSECT operator causes all unique rows selected by both individual queries to be added to the result set.
The result set contains the intersection of the two individual queries
STUDENT
TUTOR
SELECT name FROM STUDENT
INTERSECT
SELECT name FROM TUTOR;
Carol Chen
Result set only includes names found in both tables

SQL: INTERSECT & MINUS
The MINUS operator selects all unique rows selected by the first query but not the second.
The result set contains the all rows selected by the first queries that are not selected by the second query
TUTOR
STUDENT
SELECT name FROM STUDENT
MINUS
SELECT name FROM TUTOR;
Amanda Allen
Bob Black
Dave Dole
Result set only includes names found in query1 that are not in query2

SQL: Minus
SELECT name FROM TUTOR
MINUS
SELECT name FROM STUDENT;
Ed Ellis
Fred Foley
Swapping the table names will alter which rows are selected
Result set only includes names found in query1 that are not in query2
STUDENT
TUTOR

SQL: Selecting Literals
When dealing with non matching columns between the tables, you may need to utilise literal values
What is a literal value?  A literal value is a value 'hardcoded' into the query; the value is not generated by a value in the table.
Celeste	F
Fred	M
Jeni		F
Louis	M
SELECT   name,   gender
FROM    customer
WHERE  gender is NOT NULL;
SELECT   name, 'Unknown'
FROM    customer
WHERE  gender is NULL
Chris	Unknown
Lee		Unknown
Taylor	Unknown
SELECT   name, 'Unknown' FROM    customer WHERE  gender is NULL
UNION
SELECT   name, 'Unknown' FROM    customer WHERE  gender is NOT NULL
Celeste	F
Chris	Unknown
Fred	M …

SQL: Unions & Literals
Business Requirement: List all customers. If the customer has a exceeded their credit limit, then show "Bad Customer". Otherwise show "Good Customer".
SELECT	custnumb,   'Good Customer '  As Rating
FROM	customer
WHERE	balance <= credlim
UNION
SELECT	custnumb, 'Bad Customer'    As Rating
FROM	customer
WHERE	balance > credlim
ORDER BY custnumb;
CUSTNUMB
Rating
124
Good Customer
256
Good Customer
311
Good Customer
315
Bad Customer
405
Good Customer
412
Good Customer
522
Good Customer
567
Good Customer
587
Good Customer
622
Bad Customer
Literal
Result Set contains values that are not obtained from the database tables

SQL: Unique Constraints
We have already used 3 types of constraint when creating tables:
Primary Key, Foreign Key, Not Null
A unique constraint ensures that a column valueis never duplicated throughout all column values
CREATE TABLE  MOVIE (
movieno   	NUMBER
, title	VARCHAR(30)
… becomes …
CREATE TABLE  MOVIE (
movieno   	NUMBER
, title	VARCHAR(30) UNIQUE

Unique Constraint with multiple columns
A unique constraint can include more than one column
Therefore it must be created as a table constraint.
E.g. Business Rule: An actor name must never be repeated.
An actor’s name consists of values in two attributes:
CREATE TABLE ACTOR  (
actorno	NUMBER            	   PRIMARY KEY
, surname	VARCHAR(30)        NOT NULL
, firstname	VARCHAR(30)        NOT NULL
, birthdate	DATE
, UNIQUE(surname, firstname)
…

SQL: Check Constraints
A Check Constraint performs fixed validation on data values
CREATE   STUDENT (
stuid	NUMBER
, name	VARCHAR(30)
, gender	VARCHAR (1)
CHECK (gender  IN  ('M', 'F', 'I')  )
…
This check constraint limits the gender value to Male, Female or Intersex
"This recommended approach complies with the Australian Standard (AS) 4590:2006 'Interchange of client information' (a standard for making data easier to share between parties). The Australian government has recognised that intersex is a response option that needs to be provided whenever gender data is being collected. This means that increasingly, Australian government agencies must include this option in their forms and allow for it in their databases. Finally, discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation, gender identity or intersex status has also recently been declared illegal in Australia."
http://www.formulate.com.au/blog/sex-and-gender

SQL: Check Constraints
Check Constraints perform fixed validation on data values
CREATE   STUDENT (
stuid    NUMBER
, name  VARCHAR(30)
, test1   NUMBER  CHECK (test1 >= 0)
, test2  NUMBER  CHECK (test2 BETWEEN 0 and 50)
, CHECK (test1 + test2 <= 100)
…

SQL: Check Constraints
Check Constraints should only be used to validate specific values
When the values are fixed
Will never change
CHECK (dayname IN ('Mon','Tue','Wed','Thu','Fri','Sat','Sun'))
Do not use check constraints when the values may change
Adding/deleting values will require a modification to a table that may contain many of rows of data
CHECK (managername IN ('Dave', 'Sue', 'Henry')  )
CHECK (grade IN ('Good', 'Excellent', 'Poor')  )
It is better to create a separate table of managers or grades that the end-user can maintain
🗶

SQL: Check Constraint rules
Check constraint rules:
A row passes the Check Constraint rule if the result of condition is:
TRUE   or
NULL   (unknown)
The Check syntax is the same as the WHERE clause, except
Check Constraints cannot contain:
conditions that refer to values in other rows or other tables
subqueries
calls to SQL functions like SYSDATE
